In a general rotationally symmetric gauge the flow is a coupled system for the radial lapse and warping factor; a single scalar equation appears only after a Hamilton--DeTurck gauge choice. In the corresponding moving DeTurck frame the squared warping function $Ψ=Φ^2$ satisfies the linear forced heat equation \begin{equation*} D_tΨ=Ψ_{ss}-2, \end{equation*} while the fixed-lapse coordinate form contains the associated transport term. Since the Bures--Helstrom metric is Einstein, the geometric flow itself is the homothetic shrinker \begin{equation*} g(t)=(1-4t)g_{\mathrm{BH}}, \end{equation*} with scalar curvature $6/(1-4t)$ and extinction time $T=1/4$. Thus the metric remains inside the monotone cone for all $t<T$ and leaves the cone of nondegenerate Riemannian metrics only through the collapsed limit. We also record the volume--normalized flow, for which the Bures--Helstrom metric is a fixed point. Its linearization is the shifted round--sphere Laplacian $Δ_{\mathbb S^3}+3$, with spectrum \begin{equation*} σ_\ell=-(\ell-1)(\ell+3), \end{equation*} and spectral gap $5$ after removal of the scaling mode.
one_liner: The Bures-Helstrom qubit metric is shown to evolve under Ricci flow as an explicit homothetic shrinker.
why_it_matters: It clarifies the geometric flow of a core quantum Fisher-information metric, but its contribution is mainly mathematical rather than experimental photonics.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes Ricci flow for the Bures-Helstrom metric on the qubit state space, using the quantum Fisher normalization that maps the Bloch ball to a geodesic hemisphere of the round three-sphere. It derives coordinate and DeTurck-gauge descriptions, reducing the moving-frame warping function to a forced heat equation. Because the metric is Einstein, the geometric flow is an explicit homothetic shrinker with extinction time 1/4. The volume-normalized flow fixes the metric and has a linearized spectrum with a spectral gap after removing scaling.
main_result: The Bures-Helstrom metric remains in the monotone cone under Ricci flow until collapse, with normalized flow making it a fixed point.
application_angle: The result supports rigorous modeling of qubit distinguishability and Fisher-information geometry used in quantum estimation and photonic qubit analysis.

title: Passive-User Bell-State Loop-Back Key Establishment without Quantum Detectors at the User Nodes
abstract: We propose and analyze a Bell-state extension of the Loop-Back quantum key distribution architecture for secret-key establishment between two passive users that do not require quantum transmitters or quantum detectors. In the proposed setting, a single active station, Alice, provides the entangled-state infrastructure, retains one qubit of an initially prepared Bell pair, and sends the traveling subsystem through two passive users, denoted by $B_1$ and $B_2$. Each passive user applies a local Pauli operation to the same traveling subsystem, so that the operation observed by Alice is only the effective composition $U_{\mathrm{eff}}=U_2U_1$. After the subsystem returns, Alice performs a Bell-state measurement and, using her private knowledge of the initial Bell state, deterministically identifies the effective Pauli operation. However, the individual factors $U_1$ and $U_2$ remain algebraically hidden from Alice whenever the local choices are uniformly and independently selected. The public effective operation acts as a parity-like constraint: each passive user can infer the operation applied by the other from its own private choice, while the active station learns only the global composition. This construction transfers the essential distributed-transformation mechanism of passive-user Loop-Back QKD to the entangled-state regime. Unlike single-qubit passive-user schemes, whose useful events are intrinsically post-selected, the Bell-state version is limited primarily by the success probability of the Bell-state measurement. We discuss the algebraic structure of the protocol, its interpretation as an infrastructure-assisted mediated key-establishment mechanism, and the physical assumptions required to protect passive Pauli modulators against active injection or Trojan-horse-type attacks.
one_liner: A Bell-state loop-back QKD variant lets passive users establish keys without quantum transmitters or detectors at their nodes.
why_it_matters: It shifts quantum hardware complexity to one active station, making mediated key establishment more plausible for low-complexity user endpoints.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes an entangled-state extension of loop-back QKD in which Alice prepares a Bell pair, keeps one qubit, and sends the other through two passive users. Each user applies a local Pauli operation, while Alice's Bell-state measurement reveals only the effective composition of the two operations. With uniformly random local choices, the individual user operations remain hidden from Alice but can be inferred by the other user from the public effective operation. The scheme replaces user-side quantum detection and transmission with passive Pauli modulation, while highlighting required defenses against active injection and Trojan-horse attacks.
main_result: The protocol enables detector-free passive users to establish a shared key through an active Bell-state infrastructure station.
application_angle: It is relevant to quantum communication architectures where endpoint simplicity, low cost, and centralized quantum infrastructure are priorities.

title: Electrical Noise Produced by Micron-Sized Particles above a Surface Paul Trap
abstract: Electric field noise produced by the surface of ion trap electrodes reduces the fidelity of quantum computing operations. Despite decades of investigation its microscopic origins remain unclear. Here, we measure electric field noise at trapping locations along the symmetry axis of a linear surface Paul trap. We find that noise levels vary by three orders-of-magnitude in one 600$\,μ$m section of the trap. Optical and scanning electron microscope images show micron-sized particles close to the trapping locations with the highest noise levels. We find that modeling the particles as a lossy dielectric with a effective loss tangent $\tanθ=0.33(0.06)$ describes the magnitude of the noise, as well as its spatial and frequency dependence. Our observations may explain the large variation of reported noise levels in literature.
one_liner: Micron-scale particles near a surface Paul trap are linked to three-orders-of-magnitude variations in ion-trap electric-field noise.
why_it_matters: The measurements identify a concrete contamination mechanism that can limit trapped-ion gate fidelity and explain device-to-device noise scatter.
detailed_summary: The authors measure electric-field noise along the symmetry axis of a linear surface Paul trap and observe variations of three orders of magnitude within a 600 micrometer region. Optical and scanning electron microscopy correlate the highest-noise trap locations with nearby micron-sized particles. Modeling those particles as lossy dielectrics with an effective loss tangent of 0.33(0.06) reproduces the magnitude, spatial dependence, and frequency dependence of the noise. The observations provide a plausible microscopic source for anomalous heating variability across trapped-ion experiments.
main_result: Micron-sized surface particles quantitatively account for large local electric-field noise enhancements above a surface Paul trap.
application_angle: The result points to particle control, inspection, and cleaning as practical routes to improving trapped-ion quantum processor fidelity.

title: Emergency hub placement with a neutral-atom quantum computer
abstract: We study the problem of emergency operation center placement in disaster response, where a minimal number of hubs must be selected to ensure timely coverage of all affected locations. This task can be formulated as a minimum dominating set problem on a graph encoding reachability within a target response time. We propose a hybrid quantum-classical approximation framework that leverages neutral-atom quantum computers as independent set samplers. Candidate dominating sets are constructed from both small maximal independent sets and complements of large independent sets, and are subsequently refined via a lightweight classical procedure. We benchmark the approach on synthetic instances and realistic case studies, and implement it on the Fresnel quantum processor by Pasqal, solving instances of up to 100 nodes. Our results show that quantum-generated samples, despite hardware noise, enable near-optimal solutions of the placement problem. Overall, our results demonstrate that neutral-atom devices operating in analog mode can already be used to tackle graph optimization problems for real-world applications.
one_liner: A neutral-atom processor is used as an independent-set sampler for emergency hub placement instances up to 100 nodes.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates analog neutral-atom hardware applied to a real operational graph-optimization task, not just abstract benchmark graphs.
detailed_summary: The paper maps emergency operation center placement to a minimum dominating set problem on a graph representing reachability under response-time constraints. A hybrid algorithm uses a neutral-atom quantum computer as an independent-set sampler, then builds candidate dominating sets from small maximal independent sets and complements of large independent sets. A lightweight classical refinement step improves the sampled candidates. The approach is benchmarked on synthetic and realistic cases and implemented on Pasqal's Fresnel processor for instances up to 100 nodes.
main_result: Quantum-generated independent-set samples enabled near-optimal emergency hub placement solutions despite analog hardware noise.
application_angle: The work shows how current neutral-atom devices can support application-facing logistics and infrastructure optimization workflows.

title: Subsystem Quantum Error Correction for Noisy Quantum Metrology
abstract: Quantum error correction has been successfully applied to enhance the precision of parameter estimation in the presence of noise. Nonetheless, existing methods require a number of noiseless, controllable ancillae and lack efficient encoding and decoding procedures. In this Letter, we demonstrate that subsystem error correction provides a new direction that can substantially simplify the metrological protocol. We derive general conditions under which subsystem stabilizer codes achieve the Heisenberg limit and show that, for broad classes of noise, this can be realized by syndrome-free protocols using at most a single ancilla qubit. Furthermore, we extend this framework to dynamical error correction and show that Floquet codes can protect time-dependent metrological signals in reaching the Heisenberg limit.
one_liner: Subsystem stabilizer codes simplify error-corrected quantum metrology and can reach Heisenberg scaling with little or no syndrome processing.
why_it_matters: It lowers the ancilla and control overhead for noise-resilient sensing, making QEC-enhanced metrology more experimentally realistic.
detailed_summary: The paper develops subsystem quantum error correction as a route to robust parameter estimation under noise. It derives general conditions for subsystem stabilizer codes to preserve Heisenberg-limited scaling and identifies broad noise classes where syndrome-free protocols suffice. The constructions can use at most a single ancilla qubit, reducing a major resource burden in prior QEC metrology schemes. The framework is also extended to dynamical error correction, with Floquet codes protecting time-dependent signals.
main_result: Subsystem QEC can achieve Heisenberg-limited noisy metrology with simplified encoding, decoding, and ancilla requirements.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for practical quantum sensors that need error protection without large clean ancillary registers or complex real-time correction.

title: Optimized Quantum States for Sensing in the Presence of Loss and Phase Noise
abstract: Squeezed vacuum lets gravitational-wave detectors and other quantum sensors surpass the standard quantum limit, and is optimal in the loss-limited regime; phase noise breaks this optimality. Numerically optimizing the quantum Fisher information across the loss and phase-noise landscape, we identify non-Gaussian states that outperform any Gaussian state. These fall into three classes: Fock-like, cubic-phase-like, and states with discrete rotational symmetry. Limiting the average number of photons in the input state to $\bar{n}=5$, with $1-η= 5\%$ photon loss and 200 mrad phase noise, the non-Gaussian advantage reaches up to 2.2 dB. Furthermore, we observe that the non-Gaussian advantage can persist even when the measurement strategy is homodyne detection.
one_liner: Numerical QFI optimization finds non-Gaussian probe states that beat Gaussian sensing under combined photon loss and phase noise.
why_it_matters: It targets a realistic noise regime where squeezed vacuum loses optimality, relevant to interferometric sensors such as gravitational-wave detectors.
detailed_summary: The work optimizes input quantum states for phase sensing across loss and phase-noise conditions using quantum Fisher information. For mean photon number nbar=5, 5% loss, and 200 mrad phase noise, non-Gaussian probes give up to 2.2 dB advantage over all Gaussian states. The optimized states fall into Fock-like, cubic-phase-like, and discrete-rotational-symmetry classes, with some advantage retained under homodyne detection.
main_result: Non-Gaussian photonic probe states outperform Gaussian probes by up to 2.2 dB in a lossy, phase-noisy sensing regime.
application_angle: The results guide experimentally relevant probe-state engineering for precision optical metrology under realistic technical noise.

title: $K$-Theoretic Obstructions to Linearizing QCA Representations
abstract: Projective representations arise naturally in physics and representation theory, and determining whether they can be linearized has been a fundamental problem. In this work, we study the analogous problem for quantum cellular automata (QCA) representations, which incorporate locality constraints imposed by a metric space $X$. Over an arbitrary field $\mathbb{F}$, we develop an obstruction theory for the linearization of QCA representations, using the algebraic $K$-theory spectrum of QCA constructed in previous work of the authors. The resulting obstructions are governed by the homotopy type of the QCA spaces, from which we extract universal obstruction classes to linearization. In the complex algebraic and unitary case, we also fully compute the homotopy types of the QCA spaces over a point, a line, and a plane.
one_liner: The paper develops K-theoretic obstruction classes for when locality-preserving QCA representations can be linearized.
why_it_matters: Although mainly mathematical, it sharpens the classification tools behind locality-constrained quantum dynamics and topological quantum hardware models.
detailed_summary: This work studies linearization of quantum cellular automata representations under metric-space locality constraints. It builds an obstruction theory using the algebraic K-theory spectrum of QCA and extracts universal obstruction classes governed by QCA homotopy types. In complex algebraic and unitary settings, it computes QCA spaces over a point, line, and plane.
main_result: The authors formulate universal K-theoretic obstructions to linearizing QCA representations and compute key low-dimensional QCA homotopy types.
application_angle: The framework can inform rigorous classification of locality-preserving operations used in topological phases and fault-tolerant quantum architectures.

title: Design Considerations for Phase Modulation in Testable Photonic Systems and Co-packaged Optics
abstract: As silicon photonic integrated circuits (PICs) scale in complexity, testing and calibration increasingly depend on effective phase modulation mechanisms. This work compares thermally induced phase modulation and carrier-based electrical modulation in Mach-Zehnder and microring modulators. The devices are designed and evaluated for extinction ratio, tuning efficiency, power consumption, and modulation bandwidth. The study identifies key trade-offs among modulation speed, energy consumption, and tuning controllability that directly influence the suitability of these methods for test signal generation and calibration tasks. The results highlight the relative advantages and limitations of thermal and electrical approaches across different operating regimes. These findings provide practical design guidance for selecting phase modulation strategies in scalable silicon photonic systems with integrated test and calibration requirements.
one_liner: A silicon photonics study compares thermal and carrier-based phase modulation for calibration and testable co-packaged optics.
why_it_matters: Scalable PICs need built-in tuning and calibration, and this paper maps practical trade-offs among speed, power, efficiency, and controllability.
detailed_summary: The paper evaluates thermally induced and carrier-based electrical phase modulation in Mach-Zehnder and microring devices. It compares extinction ratio, tuning efficiency, power consumption, and modulation bandwidth for test-signal generation and calibration. The analysis identifies operating regimes where thermal tuning or electrical modulation is preferable for scalable silicon PICs.
main_result: The study provides device-level trade-off guidance for selecting phase modulators in testable silicon photonic and co-packaged optical systems.
application_angle: Its design rules are directly useful for built-in self-test, calibration, and control of large photonic integrated circuits.

title: Topological Quantum Interferometry
abstract: Structured light provides high-dimensional Hilbert spaces holding tremendous potential for fundamental quantum optics and quantum technologies. However, existing characterization methods, like Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference, typically assume perfectly tuned conditions, overlooking the geometric physics governing spatial mode evolution. Here, we establish topological quantum interferometry driven by an interaction-based geometric phase, the exchange Berry phase (BPX). Our formalism generalizes $q$-plate state generation and characterization to arbitrary topological charges and (de)tuning conditions, demonstrating that BPX acts as a geometric marker governing spatial interference. We show BPX serves as a deterministic control parameter, decomposing two-photon spatial patterns into geometry-dictated fundamental modes. This mapping reveals topological invariants and phase singularities that function as a non-tomographic witness for state dimensionality estimation, circumventing full-state reconstruction. Being device-independent and highly scalable, this approach enables scalable high-dimensional characterization and topologically protected state selection, with direct applicability to quantum metrology and high-capacity quantum networks.
one_liner: Topological quantum interferometry uses exchange Berry phase as a geometric marker for high-dimensional spatial-mode characterization.
why_it_matters: It offers a scalable, non-tomographic route to characterize structured-light quantum states for metrology and high-capacity quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces an interferometric formalism where an exchange Berry phase governs two-photon spatial interference under arbitrary topological charges and detuning. It generalizes q-plate state generation and characterization beyond perfectly tuned conditions. The resulting interference patterns expose topological invariants and phase singularities that can witness state dimensionality without full tomography.
main_result: Exchange Berry phase is shown to deterministically control and decompose two-photon spatial interference into geometry-dictated modes.
application_angle: The method could simplify characterization and selection of high-dimensional photonic states for quantum metrology and quantum communication networks.

title: μ-MOPA Architecture for Photonic Integrated Solid State Laser
abstract: Diode-pumped solid-state (DPSS) lasers play a central role in modern photonics owing to their exceptional efficiency and ability to extend spectral coverage beyond the reach of semiconductor diodes. These attributes have enabled breakthroughs in precision metrology, quantum optics, and coherent communications. However, bringing the proven advantages of DPSS gain media such as Nd:YAG onto an integrated photonic platform has remained difficult, largely due to inefficient pump utilization and limited power-scaling in chip-scale implementations. Here, we demonstrate the first photonic-integrated Nd:YAG laser-amplifier system that overcomes these challenges with a micro-chip based master-oscillator-power-amplifier (μ-MOPA) architecture. The seed laser, employing a double-resonant microring resonator, could reach a threshold as low as 2.9 μW. The single-pass waveguide amplifier, when optimized separately, provides up to 46.6 dB small-signal gain. Combining the low-threshold seed with cascaded waveguide amplifiers, the integrated μ-MOPA delivers more than 12 dBm of amplified continuous-wave output power. These results establish Nd:YAG waveguide integration as a practical route to compact and high-performance solid-state light sources.
one_liner: An integrated Nd:YAG micro-MOPA combines a low-threshold microring seed laser with waveguide amplifiers for >12 dBm CW output.
why_it_matters: It brings solid-state laser performance closer to chip-scale photonics for metrology, quantum optics, and coherent communication systems.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate a photonic-integrated Nd:YAG laser-amplifier system using a micro-chip master-oscillator-power-amplifier architecture. The double-resonant microring seed reaches a 2.9 uW threshold, while separately optimized single-pass waveguide amplifiers provide up to 46.6 dB small-signal gain. Cascaded integration yields more than 12 dBm continuous-wave amplified output power.
main_result: The work demonstrates a chip-scale Nd:YAG micro-MOPA delivering >12 dBm continuous-wave output from a low-threshold integrated seed and amplifiers.
application_angle: Compact, efficient solid-state sources can support deployable precision metrology, quantum-optics experiments, and coherent photonic systems.

title: Robust Generation of Topological Biphoton Mode via Adiabatic Passage
abstract: Topological waveguide arrays support robust mode propagation in the presence of fabrication imperfections, providing a significant advantage for on-chip quantum information processing. However, this robustness does not fully extend to nonlinear biphoton generation. Structural disorder can enhance the excitation of non-topological biphoton modes during nonlinear interactions, which degrades the quantum properties of the generated state. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adiabatic passage that connects an isolated site to a topological defect array. By initiating the nonlinear process in a strongly isolated regime, nonlinear coupling to unwanted modes is effectively suppressed, thereby preserving the Schmidt number of the generated state. The subsequent adiabatic connection facilitates the high fidelity transfer of the generated biphoton into the topological biphoton mode. Our numerical simulations demonstrate that, unlike conventional topological structures, the adiabatic scheme maintains both high biphoton fidelity and a unit Schmidt number in the presence of waveguide gap disorder. Furthermore, we show that this robustness extends to path entangled NOON states, achieving a near-unity quantum interference visibility. Our approach provides a practical design strategy for disorder-tolerant integrated quantum photonic devices.
one_liner: An adiabatic topological waveguide scheme robustly generates high-fidelity biphoton and NOON states despite gap disorder.
why_it_matters: It addresses a key practical weakness of nonlinear topological photonics: disorder can spoil the quantum state during generation, not just propagation.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes starting nonlinear generation in a strongly isolated site and adiabatically connecting it to a topological defect array. This suppresses nonlinear coupling into unwanted modes before transferring the generated pair into the topological biphoton mode. Simulations show high biphoton fidelity, unit Schmidt number under waveguide gap disorder, and near-unity interference visibility for path-entangled NOON states.
main_result: Adiabatic passage preserves high-fidelity topological biphoton generation and robust NOON-state interference in disordered waveguide arrays.
application_angle: The design strategy supports disorder-tolerant integrated quantum photonic sources for on-chip quantum information processing.

title: QMCtwin: Master-Equation Simulation of Syndrome Statistics Beyond Pauli Noise
abstract: As quantum error correction moves toward large-scale experimental implementations, decoder performance increasingly depends on how faithfully hardware noise is translated into syndrome statistics. Standard stabilizer workflows achieve scalability by replacing device dynamics with stochastic Pauli or detector-error models, but this compression can discard coherent phase information, nonunital drift, continuous-time effects of always-on couplings, and correlations generated by simultaneous Hamiltonian and dissipative evolution. Here we present QMCtwin, a sign-problem-suppressed quantum Monte Carlo framework for master-equation simulation of QEC circuits, and apply it to a full syndrome-extraction round of a distance-$7$ rotated surface code with $97$ physical qubits. The open-system model includes realistic superconducting-device noise mechanisms such as relaxation, pure dephasing, coherent gate miscalibration, residual $ZZ$ crosstalk, and drive-qubit detuning. By directly estimating syndrome observables from the QMC-generated stochastic density matrix estimator, we compare the master-equation dynamics with their Pauli-twirled Clifford simulation counterparts. QMCtwin predicts syndrome-extraction biases and correlations between syndromes and proxies of logical-string-parity that are absent or strongly suppressed in the stochastic Pauli description. We introduce information-theoretic diagnostics that further quantify how information concerning syndromes versus string-parity proxies differs between the realistic master-equation simulation and the corresponding Pauli-twirled model. These results show that QMC-based master-equation digital twins can expose noise features hidden by conventional Pauli/Clifford noise models and provide a practical path toward more accurate decoder-facing syndrome models.
one_liner: QMCtwin simulates surface-code syndrome extraction under realistic master-equation superconducting noise beyond Pauli approximations.
why_it_matters: It gives decoder developers a more faithful way to see coherent, nonunital, continuous-time, and correlated noise that Pauli-twirled models can miss.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces QMCtwin, a sign-problem-suppressed quantum Monte Carlo framework for estimating QEC syndrome observables from master-equation dynamics. It is applied to one syndrome-extraction round of a distance-7 rotated surface code with 97 superconducting-qubit physical qubits. The model includes relaxation, pure dephasing, coherent gate miscalibration, residual ZZ crosstalk, and drive-qubit detuning, revealing syndrome biases and correlations suppressed in Pauli-twirled Clifford simulations.
main_result: QMCtwin predicts decoder-facing syndrome biases and syndrome-to-string-parity correlations absent or strongly reduced in Pauli noise models.
application_angle: More realistic syndrome statistics can improve decoder training, hardware digital twins, and QEC performance forecasts for superconducting processors.

title: A 3D passive ring gyroscope for seismology
abstract: In seismology and related fields, the measurement of rotation in all three spatial dimensions is essential to complement the observation of translations. Access to all six degrees of freedom allows for full reconstruction of seismic wavefields and improves the understanding of complex ground motion during seismic events. In this regard, Sagnac interferometers in the form of large active ring laser systems have demonstrated remarkable performance. So-called passive ring gyroscopes offer the potential to bypass some of the limitations of active ring lasers and could represent a promising complement to existing sensor technology. Here, we present a prototype of a transportable three dimensional free-space passive ring gyroscope, reaching a sensitivity in the micro rad/s/sqrt(Hz) regime in all spatial dimensions. We demonstrate the sensor performance by reconstructing the rotational components of a simulated seismic event.
one_liner: A transportable 3D passive free-space ring gyroscope measures rotational motion for seismic wavefield reconstruction.
why_it_matters: Three-axis rotation sensing complements translational seismology and can enable full six-degree-of-freedom measurements in deployable instruments.
detailed_summary: The work presents a prototype transportable three-dimensional passive ring gyroscope based on free-space Sagnac interferometry. It reaches sensitivity in the micro-rad/s/sqrt(Hz) regime along all three spatial axes. The authors validate operation by reconstructing rotational components of a simulated seismic event, positioning passive rings as a complement to large active ring laser systems.
main_result: The prototype demonstrates three-axis rotational sensitivity in the micro-rad/s/sqrt(Hz) range and reconstructs simulated seismic rotations.
application_angle: Deployable rotation sensors could improve seismic monitoring, wavefield reconstruction, and characterization of complex ground motion.

title: On chip, multifunctional quantum sensing using single spins in a van der Waals crystal
abstract: Nanoscale thermometry and magnetometry are in high demand across a wide range of scientific and technological applications. In this context, optically addressable spins in solids have emerged at the forefront of on-chip quantum sensing. However, simultaneous quantum sensing of multiple parameters (e.g., temperature and magnetic field) using the same spin sensor remains challenging due to cross-sensitivity to multiple physical quantities. Here, we demonstrate independent dual sensing of temperature and magnetic field using single quantum emitters in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). We experimentally verify the independent response of the zero-phonon line (ZPL) position to temperature and of optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) to magnetic fields. Furthermore, we demonstrate local temperature sensing of a microcircuit while simultaneously measuring an external magnetic field. Our results establish quantum emitters in hBN as a robust platform for multifunctional quantum sensing under realistic operating conditions.
one_liner: Single quantum emitters in hBN perform independent on-chip temperature and magnetic-field sensing.
why_it_matters: It addresses cross-sensitivity in nanoscale quantum sensing by separating optical ZPL thermometry from ODMR magnetometry in one van der Waals sensor.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates multifunctional quantum sensing with single optically addressable emitters in hexagonal boron nitride. Temperature is read out through shifts of the zero-phonon-line position, while magnetic field is measured through optically detected magnetic resonance. The authors show local temperature sensing of a microcircuit while simultaneously measuring an external magnetic field under realistic operating conditions.
platform_or_system: single spins in hexagonal boron nitride
main_result: A single hBN emitter independently measures local temperature and magnetic field using distinct ZPL and ODMR observables.
application_angle: On-chip dual-parameter sensing is relevant for nanoscale diagnostics in microelectronics, materials, and integrated quantum devices.

title: QPU-scale randomized benchmarking via Bell-pair injection
abstract: Mirror randomized benchmarking (MRB) is an established technique that provides a global error metric at the scale of a whole QPU. To expand upon this we introduce Mirror Quantum Awesomeness (MQA), a hybrid protocol that adds a structured entangling layer to MRB circuits. This enables per-edge correlation dynamics to be tracked via mutual information while preserving the MRB infidelity estimate. The resulting analysis of the injected entangled pairs locates a critical circuit depth, beyond which rudimentary error mitigation techniques can be expected to fail. A topological variant, Topological MQA, supplies a second critical depth via a decoder based on the surface-code decoding problem. Both are validated in simulation and demonstrated on the 156-qubit \texttt{ibm\_fez} and \texttt{ibm\_kingston} processors, where MQA closely agrees with MRB on the entanglement infidelity and the critical depth for \texttt{ibm\_fez} is found to be $\sim 50$.
one_liner: Bell-pair injection extends mirror randomized benchmarking to track QPU-scale entanglement errors and per-edge correlations.
why_it_matters: It turns global benchmarking into a more diagnostic hardware test while retaining an MRB-style infidelity estimate on 156-qubit IBM processors.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce Mirror Quantum Awesomeness, a hybrid protocol that injects structured Bell-pair entanglement layers into mirror randomized benchmarking circuits. The protocol preserves a global entanglement infidelity estimate while using mutual information to track per-edge correlation dynamics. Simulations and runs on 156-qubit ibm_fez and ibm_kingston processors show agreement with MRB and identify a critical depth of about 50 for ibm_fez, with a topological variant adding a decoder-derived critical depth.
main_result: MQA benchmarks whole-QPU entanglement infidelity while locating correlation dynamics and critical circuit depths on ibm_fez and ibm_kingston.
application_angle: The method can guide processor calibration, connectivity-aware diagnostics, and expectations for when simple error mitigation will fail.

title: Multi-objective design of photon blockade for bright single-photon sources
abstract: High-quality single-photon sources, realized through saturable emitters, photon blockade, or heralded pair generation, are indispensable building blocks for photonic quantum platforms. Although these mechanisms suppress multiphoton emission through distinct principles typically captured by analytical models, their practical implementation is constrained by conflicting requirements for purity, brightness, and indistinguishability, which must be balanced within high-dimensional design landscapes. Here, we propose a computational framework for optimizing competing metrics of single-photon sources. Building on a Liouville-space adjoint formulation that efficiently evaluates multiple objectives in Markovian open quantum systems, we develop a Jacobian-based update, which ensures first-order monotonic reduction of multi-objective costs. By incorporating simulated annealing to escape gradient-vanishing plateaus, our framework achieves a design success rate of nearly 60 % for photon blockade with g2(0) smaller than 0.1 and theoretically bounded brightness across a broad parameter space, without any analytical guidance. This framework provides a general recipe for multi-objective design of open quantum systems.
one_liner: A multi-objective adjoint optimization framework designs bright photon-blockade single-photon sources without analytic guidance.
why_it_matters: It directly tackles the brightness, purity, and indistinguishability tradeoffs that constrain practical quantum-photonic source engineering.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a computational design framework for Markovian open quantum systems using a Liouville-space adjoint formulation for multiple source metrics. A Jacobian-based update enforces first-order monotonic reduction of multi-objective costs, while simulated annealing helps escape gradient-vanishing plateaus. For photon blockade, the approach achieves nearly 60 percent design success for g2(0) below 0.1 with theoretically bounded brightness across broad parameter ranges.
main_result: The optimizer finds high-purity photon-blockade designs with g2(0) below 0.1 and bounded brightness across a broad design space.
application_angle: Automated multi-metric design can accelerate practical single-photon source development for photonic quantum computing and networking.

title: Applications of quantum annealing to magnetic dipole hyperfine structure constants: First results beyond energies for atoms
abstract: We report the first results of the magnetic dipole hyperfine structure (HFS) constants of neutral $\mathrm{Li}$, Li-like $\mathrm{Be}$, neutral $\mathrm{Na}$, and Na-like $\mathrm{Mg}$ using a modified version of the Quantum Annealer Eigensolver (QAE) algorithm on D-Wave's quantum hardware. The results are benchmarked against relativistic configuration interaction with multiconfiguration Dirac Hartree-Fock (MCDHF) calculations using the General-purpose Relativistic Atomic Structure Package (GRASP), and simulated annealing. In our modified QAE, a zooming-and-sigma-annealing approach with a floating-point encoding scheme is adopted to estimate the ground-state eigenvalue and eigenvector of the relativistic Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian matrices ($H_{\mathrm{DC}}$) constructed from 11 or fewer configuration state functions (CSFs). For calculations with extended correlation orbital sets, we applied a CSF truncation scheme, retaining only CSFs (up to 12) that make significant contributions to the ground-state wavefunction. Our modified QAE precision is kept limited to three decimal places (up to 10 qubits). Hardware demonstrations on the D-Wave quantum processing unit (QPU) yielded results that were completely consistent with GRASP (at the chosen precision) in determining the magnetic dipole HFS constants, with accuracy varying across systems and $H_{\mathrm{DC}}$ matrix dimensions.
one_liner: D-Wave quantum annealing computes atomic magnetic dipole hyperfine constants beyond ground-state energies.
why_it_matters: It is an early hardware demonstration of quantum annealing for atomic observables, not just eigenvalue estimation.
detailed_summary: The authors apply a modified Quantum Annealer Eigensolver on D-Wave hardware to estimate magnetic dipole hyperfine structure constants for Li, Li-like Be, Na, and Na-like Mg. Their method uses zooming-and-sigma annealing with floating-point encoding to solve small relativistic Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian matrices, with CSF truncation for extended correlation spaces. At three-decimal precision and up to 10 qubits, hardware results are consistent with GRASP benchmarks at the chosen precision, with accuracy depending on system and matrix dimension.
main_result: The modified QAE estimates magnetic dipole hyperfine constants on quantum annealing hardware consistently with GRASP at the selected precision.
application_angle: This broadens near-term quantum annealer use cases toward computational atomic physics observables relevant to spectroscopy and precision modeling.

title: Optimal multi-spectral squeezing via deterministic 2D-phase optimization
abstract: Optimization routines are ubiquitous in quantum information technologies and essential to reach the resource levels required by quantum protocols. Specifically, multi-spectral squeezing for use in such protocols requires that losses be kept minimal at every stage, including coherent detection, which is performed by interfering the signal with a classical local-oscillator beam. This in turn requires control over all optical degrees of freedom of the beam in order to optimize the detection. The most general framework for this optimization relies on agnostic, off-the-shelf machine-learning techniques. Here we take the opposite approach: by focusing on a physical description of the specific optical process, we develop a deterministic sequential algorithm that provably reaches the global maximum of the visibility in a pixel basis and scales linearly with the number of pixels, thereby offering an efficient and theoretically grounded alternative to black-box optimization. In our waveguide-based setup, the optimized mask increases the visibility from 76% to 84%, corresponding to a 20% gain in mode-matching efficiency. Multi-spectral squeezing measurements confirm that this improvement translates directly into quantum readout: for the most squeezed spectral mode, the squeezing increases from $-2.08$ dB to $-2.64$ dB, consistent with the inferred efficiency gain. These results establish deterministic spatial phase shaping as an effective, interpretable route to enhanced multimode squeezing in waveguide platforms.
one_liner: Deterministic 2D phase-mask optimization improves multimode squeezing readout in a waveguide source without black-box learning.
why_it_matters: Better spatial mode matching directly recovers lost squeezing, giving a practical control method for multimode quantum photonic measurements.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a sequential phase-only optimization algorithm for maximizing signal-local-oscillator visibility in a pixel basis, with a proof of global optimality and linear scaling in pixel count. In a waveguide squeezing setup, the optimized spatial phase mask raises visibility from 76% to 84%, implying a 20% mode-matching efficiency gain. The improvement is confirmed in quantum readout, where the best spectral-mode squeezing improves from -2.08 dB to -2.64 dB.
main_result: A deterministic phase-shaping routine increases optical visibility and measurably improves the observed squeezing of a multispectral waveguide source.
application_angle: The method can improve practical homodyne detection efficiency in continuous-variable quantum photonics without relying on slow, opaque optimization loops.

title: Vine Codes: Low-Overhead Quantum LDPC Codes on a Planar Square Grid
abstract: The surface code is a promising route towards large-scale quantum computing, requiring only nearest-neighbour gates amenable to superconducting hardware. However, surface codes incur large qubit overheads. Novel quantum low-density parity check (qLDPC) codes promise to reduce overheads but require long-range connections that are difficult to achieve on superconducting platforms. Here, we introduce "Vine Codes" - qLDPC codes that are implementable on a planar square grid through nearest-neighbour, two-qubit gates native to superconducting platforms (iSWAP and CZ). Our approach generalises "Directional Codes" recently introduced by Gehér et. al. (2025) which are constrained to a torus. In contrast, vine codes have open boundary conditions constructed with the aid of routing qubits. We perform extensive numeric searches and find promising candidate vine codes, e.g. [[121,4,6]], [[221,6,7]], and [[234,9,6]] codes. We verify the circuit distances and show that data and measure qubits required can be reduced by up to ~28% relative to the surface code at a circuit distance of 7. Even including routing qubits, vine codes require fewer total qubits than the surface code (e.g. ~18% reduction at circuit distance 10) and benefits are expected to increase at higher distances. We perform circuit-level noise simulations to demonstrate that under a realistic noise model and at a near-term noise rate of $10^{-3}$, vine codes can perform better than the surface code while using fewer qubits. We give an exhaustive list of all unique vine codes up to stabiliser-weight 9. We additionally introduce "Flip-Vine Codes" which possess single-qubit transversal Clifford gates useful for fault-tolerant logic and magic state cultivation. We furthermore construct examples of generalised open boundaries for vine codes that go beyond the familiar X/Z boundaries of the surface and tile codes.
one_liner: Vine codes propose planar qLDPC layouts using nearest-neighbor superconducting gates with lower overhead than surface codes.
why_it_matters: They target the key hardware bottleneck for qLDPC codes: preserving overhead gains while staying compatible with square-grid superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: The work introduces Vine Codes, qLDPC codes with open boundaries implementable on a planar square grid using native nearest-neighbor iSWAP and CZ gates plus routing qubits. Numerical searches identify candidates such as [[121,4,6]], [[221,6,7]], and [[234,9,6]], with verified circuit distances. Simulations under circuit-level noise indicate better-than-surface-code performance at noise rate 1e-3 while using fewer qubits, and Flip-Vine variants add transversal Clifford gates.
main_result: The authors construct and simulate planar nearest-neighbor qLDPC codes that reduce total qubit counts versus surface codes at comparable circuit distance.
application_angle: If experimentally realizable, these layouts could lower the qubit overhead for fault-tolerant superconducting quantum computers.

title: Dephasingless laser wakefield acceleration in a plasma waveguide
abstract: Laser wakefield accelerators (LWFAs) provide extremely large accelerating gradients for compact electron accelerators and photon sources but are limited by dephasing, where trapped electrons outrun the accelerating phase of the wakefield. Flying-focus pulses can eliminate dephasing by driving a wake at the vacuum speed of light, but these pulses involve tradeoffs such as varying spot size, long duration, or large plasma volume. Here we show that a spatiotemporally structured laser pulse propagating in a plasma waveguide can drive a wakefield at the vacuum speed of light while maintaining a constant spot size and ultrashort duration. The pulse is formed by superposing plasma-waveguide modes with appropriately selected frequencies. Compared with flying-focus approaches, the waveguide substantially reduces the required plasma volume. Scaling laws and quasi-3D particle-in-cell simulations show that the single-stage energy gain increases linearly with the number of modes used to construct the pulse, enabling larger energy gains or shorter stages than standard LWFA.
one_liner: A plasma-waveguide mode superposition drives dephasingless laser wakefield acceleration with fixed spot size and ultrashort duration.
why_it_matters: It offers a route to higher-energy compact accelerators without the volume and pulse-shaping tradeoffs of flying-focus schemes.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes forming a spatiotemporally structured laser pulse by superposing plasma-waveguide modes with selected frequencies. This pulse drives a wakefield at the vacuum speed of light, suppressing electron dephasing while maintaining constant spot size and ultrashort duration. Scaling laws and quasi-3D particle-in-cell simulations show single-stage energy gain increasing linearly with the number of modes.
platform_or_system: structured laser pulse in plasma waveguide
main_result: Simulations show that guided multimode laser pulses can sustain dephasingless wakefield acceleration with energy gain scaling linearly in mode count.
application_angle: The approach could enable shorter, higher-gradient accelerator stages for compact electron beams and photon sources.

title: Quantum Batteries as Work Sources for Phase-Locked Parametric Amplification
abstract: Quantum batteries have been proposed as locally precharged work sources for superconducting quantum technologies, suggesting a route to reduce continuously supplied microwave drives. Here we ask whether the pump tone of a quantum-limited parametric amplifier can be replaced, or strongly duty-cycled, by a finite bosonic quantum battery. Quantizing the pump of a nondegenerate parametric amplifier exposes a resource distinction hidden in the classical description: stored pump energy can generate signal-idler photons, but pump phase coherence is required to generate a phase-locked amplifier field. In a closed trilinear model, coherent and phase-randomized coherent pumps with the same photon-number distribution produce comparable pair numbers, yet only the coherent pump produces anomalous two-mode coherence and an EPR-squeezed interference dip. Including leakage, we collect the emitted fields into cascaded temporal modes. At matched collector bandwidth, the coherent pump gives \(I_{\min}^{(f)}=0.553\), whereas the phase-randomized pump gives \(I_{\min}^{(f)}=1.94\) at nearly identical collected energy. Weak amplitude squeezing slightly improves the dip by reducing finite-pump number fluctuations while preserving the coherent displacement. Thus battery-powered parametric amplification requires phase-coherent stored energy, possibly assisted by number-noise reduction, rather than stored energy alone.
one_liner: A quantized-pump model shows quantum-battery-powered parametric amplification needs stored phase coherence, not just energy.
why_it_matters: It clarifies the resource requirement for replacing or duty-cycling microwave pumps in superconducting quantum amplifiers.
detailed_summary: The authors quantize the pump in a nondegenerate parametric amplifier and compare coherent and phase-randomized finite bosonic pumps with identical photon-number statistics. Both can generate similar signal-idler pair numbers, but only the coherent pump produces anomalous two-mode coherence and an EPR-squeezed interference dip. With leakage into temporal modes, the coherent pump gives I_min^(f)=0.553 while the phase-randomized pump gives 1.94 at nearly equal collected energy.
main_result: The study finds that phase-coherent stored pump energy is required for phase-locked quantum-limited amplification from a finite battery.
application_angle: This sets design constraints for battery-assisted superconducting readout chains and low-duty-cycle microwave drive architectures.

title: Effective Faraday interaction between light and Helium-3 nuclear spins in a multi-pass cell
abstract: Helium-3 nuclear spins form an exceptionally stable quantum system with extremely long coherence time, offering exciting opportunities for quantum technologies. In particular, nuclear spin-squeezed states promise enhanced precision for sensing tasks and tests of new physics. A central challenge for all these applications is the realization of a controllable light-nuclear spin interface. Here we experimentally demonstrate such an interface by exploiting metastability-exchange collisions in a low-pressure helium-3 gas cell at room temperature. A radio-frequency discharge produces a small population of metastable atoms that both enables efficient optical pumping and mediates an effective Faraday interaction between the collective nuclear spin and an optical probe. We quantitatively characterize the strength of this interaction as a function of the nuclear polarization, applied magnetic field, and probe-beam parameters. Moreover, we show that using a multi-pass cell enhances this interaction by effectively increasing the optical depth. Extrapolating to a tenfold increase of the probe power used in the present experiment, we project a measurement-induced squeezing rate of 0.52 s$^{-1}$. Our results provide a practical pathway for optical access to helium-3 nuclear spins and open prospects for generating long-lived, macroscopic nuclear spin-squeezed states for quantum metrology.
one_liner: A room-temperature multi-pass cell realizes an effective optical Faraday interface to long-lived helium-3 nuclear spins.
why_it_matters: Optical access to stable nuclear spins is a key missing ingredient for long-lived spin squeezing and precision quantum sensors.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses metastability-exchange collisions in low-pressure helium-3 to mediate an effective Faraday interaction between collective nuclear spins and an optical probe. A radio-frequency discharge creates metastable atoms for optical pumping and light-spin coupling at room temperature. The interaction strength is mapped versus nuclear polarization, magnetic field, and probe parameters, and a multi-pass cell boosts the effective optical depth.
main_result: The work demonstrates and characterizes a controllable light-nuclear-spin interface, projecting a measurement-induced squeezing rate of 0.52 s^-1 at higher probe power.
application_angle: It provides a practical path toward macroscopic, long-lived nuclear spin-squeezed states for magnetometry and fundamental-physics searches.

title: Observation of alignment tensor effects in metastability-exchange collisions with highly polarized 3He ensembles
abstract: Highly polarized 3He ensembles prepared by metastability-exchange optical pumping (MEOP) have been widely used in precision measurements and fundamental physics. Metastability-exchange (ME) collisions, serving as the basis of MEOP, are traditionally described in terms of atomic orientation, while the significant contributions of metastable alignment tensor at high polarization remain unexplored. In this work, we develop a linearized model under mean-field approximation to investigate alignment tensor effects in highly polarized 3He , which originate from the metastable F = 3/2 manifold and are revealed through ME-induced relaxation and frequency shift. By means of free-induction-decay (FID) measurements, a pronounced dependence on nuclear polarization is experimentally observed in the response of the ground-state-metastable hybrid 3He ensembles to the external magnetic field. Furthermore, after obtaining the characteristics of tensor-induced phenomena, we demonstrate good agreement between the experiment and the theory. This work advances the understanding of nuclear spin dynamics in highly polarized 3He using MEOP. It further provides applications in systematic error correction of high-accuracy magnetometry, as well as in optimal protocol for the generation of nuclear spin-squeezed states.
one_liner: Experiments reveal alignment-tensor effects in highly polarized helium-3 metastability-exchange spin dynamics.
why_it_matters: Accounting for these tensor shifts can reduce systematic errors in helium-3 precision sensing and improve spin-squeezing protocols.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a linearized mean-field model including metastable alignment tensor effects from the F=3/2 manifold in highly polarized helium-3. Free-induction-decay measurements show polarization-dependent relaxation and frequency shifts in ground-state-metastable hybrid ensembles under external magnetic fields. The observed tensor-induced behavior agrees well with the model after extracting the relevant characteristics.
main_result: The authors observe and model alignment-tensor-induced relaxation and frequency shifts in metastability-exchange helium-3 ensembles.
application_angle: The results support systematic correction in high-accuracy helium-3 magnetometers and better protocols for nuclear spin squeezing.

title: Phase locking nuclear spins in silicon with spin-orbit coupling
abstract: Because they have such long coherence times, nuclear spins have extraordinary potential for use in quantum information processing devices. However, coherent nuclear spin control generally requires external phase references, such as microwave control fields. Here, we phase-lock a $^{29}$Si nuclear spin ensemble in a silicon quantum dot using only the internal electronic spin-orbit coupling as a phase reference. When driven with the quantum-dot electrons, the nuclear spins align themselves to a phase determined by the electronic spin-orbit coupling and the timing of the drive protocol. This enables us to measure the coherent precession and inhomogeneous dephasing of the nuclear spins. We corroborate our results with detailed numerical simulations of the many-body electron nuclear system. Our work opens new routes for coherently controlling solid-state nuclear spin ensembles.
one_liner: Silicon quantum-dot electrons phase-lock a 29Si nuclear-spin ensemble using internal spin-orbit coupling as the phase reference.
why_it_matters: It points to coherent nuclear-spin control in silicon without external microwave phase references, useful for dense solid-state quantum memories and processors.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates phase locking of a 29Si nuclear-spin ensemble in a silicon quantum dot by driving the quantum-dot electrons. The nuclear-spin phase is set by the electronic spin-orbit coupling and drive timing, enabling measurement of coherent precession and inhomogeneous dephasing. Detailed many-body electron-nuclear simulations support the observed locking behavior.
platform_or_system: 29Si nuclear spins in a silicon quantum dot
main_result: The authors phase-lock a silicon nuclear-spin ensemble using only internal electronic spin-orbit coupling as the phase reference.
application_angle: Removing external phase references could simplify integrated silicon spin-qubit hardware and nuclear-spin ensemble control.

title: Quantum ring all-reduce: communication and privacy advantages for distributed learning
abstract: Machine learning models have scaled to unprecedented sizes, making training across distributed devices the de facto standard in the field. In this work, we explore how quantum communications can make distributed training both more communication-efficient and information-theoretically private, for both classical and quantum learning models. Ring all-reduce is the foundational communication primitive for large-scale distributed training. We present a quantum version that reduces per-link online communication by a provably optimal factor of two using pre-shared entanglement and superdense coding, without requiring the learning model or gradient computation to change. Beyond bandwidth, the primitive enables privacy guarantees that are information-theoretically impossible for any classical protocol, achieving composable ε-secure aggregation, via verified entanglement, at a 2x overhead in GHZ copies. Our hybrid quantum-classical communication architecture yields simultaneous communication and security advantages for large scale distributed training, regardless of whether the learning itself is quantum or classical. Finally, we characterise quantum advantages in gradient conflict detection for server-to-client communication under bandwidth constraints, a setting that arises after ring all-reduce is completed, when full gradient broadcast to external clients is infeasible. Two variants of the problem admit different separations. For margin-based alignment testing (\textsc{GapIP}_τ), the quantum advantage is quadratic in the margin parameter: \widetilde{O}(τ^{-1}\log P) qubits versus \widetilde{O}(\min(\τ^{-2},P)) bits. For sign-consistency auditing against a private parameter matching (\textsc{TieAudit}_ε), the advantage represents an exponential separation in communication complexity: Ω(\sqrt{P}) bits whereas O(ε^{-2}\log P) qubits suffice.
one_liner: A quantum ring all-reduce protocol uses entanglement and superdense coding to halve per-link online communication for distributed learning.
why_it_matters: It maps quantum communication primitives onto a core ML training bottleneck while adding information-theoretic privacy unavailable classically.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a quantum version of ring all-reduce for distributed training that preserves the learning model and gradient computation. With pre-shared entanglement and superdense coding, it achieves a provably optimal factor-of-two reduction in per-link online communication. Verified entanglement also enables composable epsilon-secure aggregation, and the authors analyze quantum advantages for post-reduction gradient conflict tests.
main_result: The protocol halves online per-link communication and supports information-theoretically secure aggregation using verified entanglement.
application_angle: The approach could make quantum links useful for bandwidth-limited, privacy-sensitive distributed ML infrastructure.

title: Effective discrete-modulated continuous variable QKD under general attacks
abstract: Continuous variable quantum key distribution via discrete modulations ensures information-theoretic security using standard telecom technologies, providing affordable and scalable quantum communications with simplified classical postprocessing. However, existing security proofs against general attacks often rely on restrictive assumptions, such as a bounded dimension for coherent states, or require impractically large block sizes. In this work, we develop a finite-size security analysis that removes these limitations while incorporating realistic experimental features. Our approach combines the dimension reduction technique, a security proof based on the marginal-constrained entropy accumulation, and a trusted detector model accounting for the receiver imperfections. We report positive key rates in the finite-size regime for relevant block sizes of the order of $10^8$. These results contribute to narrowing the gap between theoretical security proofs and practical implementations of discrete-modulated continuous variable quantum key distribution protocols.
one_liner: A finite-size security proof makes discrete-modulated CV-QKD viable against general attacks at practical block sizes.
why_it_matters: It removes restrictive proof assumptions and reports positive finite-size key rates around 10^8 signals, closer to deployable telecom QKD.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a finite-size security analysis for discrete-modulated continuous-variable QKD under general attacks. Their method combines dimension reduction, marginal-constrained entropy accumulation, and a trusted detector model for receiver imperfections. The analysis avoids bounded coherent-state dimension assumptions and gives positive key rates for experimentally relevant block sizes of order 10^8.
main_result: The paper proves finite-size security with realistic detector modeling and positive key rates at practical block lengths.
application_angle: This narrows the gap between rigorous security proofs and affordable telecom-compatible CV-QKD deployments.

title: Plasma Etch Process Optimization for Photonic-Grade Diamond-on-Insulator Substrates and Thickness Evaluation using Colorimetry
abstract: Diamond color-center qubits integrated with photonic circuits can be initialized, manipulated, entangled, and read individually with high fidelity, making them attractive for large-scale modular quantum computers, quantum networks, and distributed quantum sensing. However, the limited size of heteroepitaxially grown single-crystal diamond (SCD) and photonic-grade diamond-on-insulator (DOI) substrates remains a challenge for integration with existing manufacturing processes. Here, we develop a plasma etch recipe to thin direct-bonded (100) SCD membranes (<50 $μ$m) into large-area, thin-film DOI substrates, and demonstrate free-standing photonic chiplets fabricated from the resulting DOI. The ICP-RIE recipe preserves diamond bonding, provides sufficient micromasking and surface-quality control, and enables thin-film DOI manufacture. We thin a 10 $μ$m diamond plate bonded to SiO$_2$/Si and obtain a photonic-grade DOI substrate with diamond thickness $\leq$300 nm. The DOI film is around 300 nm thick over 0.5 $\times$ 0.5 mm$^2$, with surface roughness < 0.5 nm, while the bonding interface remains intact. Diamond photonic chiplets are fabricated on this DOI substrate using a standard two-step lithography process, without complex thin-film transfer, under-etching, or pedestal formation. We also present a colorimetric study of diamond visibility on SiO$_2$ and quantify color differences across thicknesses in common colorimetric spaces. This analysis enables automatic diamond-thickness extrapolation from standard optical microscope images with 5 nm resolution, in good agreement with white-light interferometry (WLI) measurements. The DOI substrate and colorimetric thickness-evaluation method provide an effective fabrication platform and reliable validation route for scalable manufacturing of diamond nanophotonic devices, opening a path toward large-scale integrated quantum systems.
one_liner: Optimized ICP-RIE thinning produces photonic-grade diamond-on-insulator films and a microscope colorimetry thickness check.
why_it_matters: Large-area DOI with sub-nm roughness and simple thickness metrology supports scalable diamond color-center photonic devices.
detailed_summary: The paper reports a plasma etch recipe for thinning direct-bonded single-crystal diamond membranes into thin-film diamond-on-insulator substrates while preserving the bonding interface. A 10 um diamond plate on SiO2/Si is thinned to roughly 300 nm over a 0.5 x 0.5 mm2 region with surface roughness below 0.5 nm, and photonic chiplets are fabricated using standard two-step lithography. The authors also quantify diamond visibility in color spaces to infer thickness from optical microscope images with 5 nm resolution, matching white-light interferometry.
main_result: The work demonstrates photonic-grade DOI fabrication and 5 nm-resolution optical colorimetry for diamond thickness evaluation.
application_angle: It provides a manufacturable substrate and inspection route for integrated diamond quantum photonics, networks, and sensing chips.

title: Computing noise-canceling observables via Pauli propagation
abstract: The pursuit of quantum advantage is driving the co-evolution of quantum processors and classical simulation methods. Despite advances in scale and quality, the accuracy of quantum simulation is ultimately limited by error rates and sampling overheads. Similarly, while classical simulation methods such as Pauli propagation have made remarkable progress, their accuracy is ultimately limited by the exponential growth of operator paths and the truncations needed to control memory and runtime. Here we show that these complementary limitations can be mitigated by embedding Pauli propagation within a hybrid error-mitigation framework that reduces quantum sampling overhead while achieving lower truncation errors with fewer classical resources than traditional Pauli propagation alone. In this framework, a target observable is classically propagated through noise-canceling inverse channels, producing a modified observable that is measured directly on a quantum processor. We prototype two implementations and benchmark their performance numerically on canonical models that challenge traditional Pauli propagation. We also perform experiments on a quantum processor using 56 superconducting qubits, revealing the tradeoffs of their respective truncation strategies. These results illustrate how classical and quantum resources can be orchestrated to extend observable estimation beyond the limits of either approach alone, providing a foundation for quantum-centric supercomputing and future demonstrations of quantum advantage.
one_liner: Hybrid Pauli propagation creates noise-canceling observables measured on a 56-qubit superconducting processor.
why_it_matters: It combines classical observable propagation with hardware measurements to reduce sampling overhead and truncation error in noisy quantum simulation.
detailed_summary: The paper embeds Pauli propagation into a hybrid error-mitigation framework by classically propagating a target observable through noise-canceling inverse channels. The resulting modified observable is then measured directly on quantum hardware, reducing quantum sampling demands while controlling classical truncation cost. The authors prototype two implementations, benchmark them numerically on challenging models, and run experiments on 56 superconducting qubits to expose truncation tradeoffs.
main_result: The authors demonstrate noise-canceling observable estimation using Pauli propagation on superconducting hardware.
application_angle: The method could extend useful observable estimation for near-term quantum simulation and quantum-centric supercomputing workflows.

title: Space-time duality approach to (inhomogeneous) integrable quenches
abstract: Characterising the universal aspects of non-equilibrium quantum many-body dynamics is one of the key goals of this century's physics research. Progress, however, is hindered by the lack of general theoretical frameworks for studying interacting quantum matter far from equilibrium. A recent breakthrough has been the realization that several key non-equilibrium quantities, such as the rate of growth of entanglement or the fluctuations of conserved charges within finite subsystems, can be related to equilibrium properties through a space-time duality that effectively exchanges the roles of space and time. This observation effectively enables the study of non-equilibrium phenomena using tools and concepts borrowed from equilibrium statistical mechanics and thermodynamics. A first proof of principle of this framework, dubbed space-time duality approach (SDA), was provided by interacting integrable systems, where thermodynamic properties can often be characterized exactly, while dynamical quantities typically remain beyond analytical reach. Subsequent developments, however, revealed that the SDA suffered from an intrinsic ambiguity, restricting its applicability to homogeneous quenches and to charge fluctuations arising from symmetric initial states. Here we resolve this ambiguity from first principles and derive closed-form predictions for entanglement growth and charge fluctuations after general quantum quenches. We benchmark our results against the exact analytical solution of the Rule 54 quantum cellular automaton and extensive TEBD simulations of the XXZ chain. Moreover we show that, when specialised to the entanglement entropy, our framework naturally reproduces the predictions of the quasiparticle picture.
one_liner: A space-time duality framework predicts entanglement growth and charge fluctuations after general integrable quantum quenches.
why_it_matters: Although theoretical, it gives experimentally relevant predictions for non-equilibrium many-body dynamics probed in quantum simulators and sensing platforms.
detailed_summary: The work resolves an intrinsic ambiguity in the space-time duality approach that had limited it to homogeneous quenches and symmetric charge-fluctuation settings. From first principles, it derives closed-form predictions for entanglement growth and charge fluctuations after general quantum quenches. The predictions are benchmarked against the exact Rule 54 quantum cellular automaton and TEBD simulations of the XXZ chain, and reduce to quasiparticle-picture results for entanglement entropy.
platform_or_system: integrable quantum spin chains and cellular automata
main_result: The paper generalizes space-time duality predictions to inhomogeneous and asymmetric integrable quench dynamics.
application_angle: The framework can guide interpretation of non-equilibrium dynamics measurements in programmable many-body quantum simulators.

title: Fidelity bounds for adiabatic gates and other quantum operations with time-dependent dissipation
abstract: As quantum-computing platforms are susceptible to noise, the fidelity of quantum operations is limited by decoherence. Understanding this limitation is crucial for building utility-scale quantum processors. In previous works [Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 150504 (2022); Quantum 9, 1684 (2025)], we presented analytical formulae for the average gate fidelity of multi-qubit operations under static Markovian noise processes, including operations that temporarily leave the computational subspace. However, some quantum-computing architectures dynamically modulate qubit or coupler frequencies to implement two-qubit gates, e.g., baseband flux gates; such modulation can lead to dissipation rates varying in time. In this Letter, we therefore generalize the fidelity-reduction formulae to encompass time-dependent dissipation. Applying our generalized formula, we obtain a fidelity bound for adiabatic operations and demonstrate that flux-dependent noise sensitivity, combined with qubit-coupler hybridization, significantly reduces the fidelity of adiabatic controlled-Z (CZ) gates in superconducting quantum computers. Our work thus provides essential theoretical tools for evaluating error budgets and optimizing the design of quantum operations in tunable quantum-computing architectures, and may also find applications in quantum-sensing and quantum-communication protocols that are affected by time-dependent dissipation.
one_liner: Generalizes quantum-operation fidelity bounds to time-dependent dissipation in tunable architectures.
why_it_matters: Gives hardware designers analytic error-budget tools for gates whose noise changes during flux modulation or adiabatic evolution.
detailed_summary: The paper extends prior average-gate-fidelity formulae from static Markovian noise to operations with dissipation rates that vary in time. It applies the result to adiabatic controlled-Z gates in superconducting circuits, where flux-dependent noise sensitivity and qubit-coupler hybridization reduce achievable fidelity. The framework covers operations that may temporarily leave the computational subspace, making it relevant to tunable coupler and baseband flux-gate designs.
main_result: Derives fidelity-reduction bounds for quantum operations under time-dependent dissipation and applies them to adiabatic superconducting CZ gates.
application_angle: The formulas can guide gate-shape optimization, coupler design, and error budgeting for scalable superconducting quantum processors.

title: General circuit mapping algorithm for neutral atom quantum computers
abstract: Neutral atom quantum computers (NAQC) are emerging as a promising, scalable quantum computing platform because of their long qubit coherence, flexible qubit arrangement, and multiqubit gate capabilities. However, circuit execution often requires physically moving qubits, making compilation a critical optimization challenge. We propose a circuit independent mathematical framework built on graph-theoretic combinatorial optimization that determines the minimal number of required qubit transfers. This model captures spatial constraints specific to NAQC platforms with zone-limited gate operations and multi-qubit gates. From this framework, we encode the qubit mapping problem as a nonlinear integer program and solve it using a genetic algorithm, enabling trade-offs between minimizing the total traveled distance and the number of parallel transfer operations. Compared to the state-of-the-art scalable compiler for zoned architectures, our approach consistently finds fewer transfers. Depending on the optimization focus, our method produces shorter traveled distances or fewer parallel transfer operations. This work provides both theoretical guaranties and a practical tool for efficient, architecture-aware quantum circuit compilation. As a result, practitioners can generate hardware-aware mappings that reduce movement-induced errors and better exploit atom transfer parallelism, directly improving execution efficiency on NAQC devices.
one_liner: Introduces a graph-optimization compiler for minimizing atom transfers in neutral-atom quantum computers.
why_it_matters: Reducing qubit movement can lower movement-induced errors and improve throughput on zoned neutral-atom processors.
detailed_summary: The work formulates neutral-atom circuit mapping as a graph-theoretic combinatorial optimization problem with zone-limited gates and multiqubit operations. It encodes the task as a nonlinear integer program and solves it with a genetic algorithm that can trade total travel distance against parallel transfer count. Compared with a scalable zoned-architecture compiler, the method consistently finds mappings requiring fewer transfers.
main_result: Demonstrates a circuit-independent mapping framework that reduces required atom transfers and optimizes transport distance or parallel movement operations.
application_angle: The compiler is directly useful for architecture-aware execution on neutral-atom devices where transport overhead and motion errors limit performance.

title: Approximating optimal decoding of quantum LDPC codes with narrow frontiers
abstract: We introduce the Frontier decoder, a pruned dynamic-programming decoder for sparse quantum decoding problems. Frontier processes error variables in a chosen order, merges prefixes with the same residual syndrome and logical label, and approximates logical-coset posterior masses by retaining only a narrow scored frontier. Without pruning, the recursion is exact ordered inference with exponential complexity. In the code-capacity setting, the decoder reaches thresholds close to optimal for the surface code and the color code. In the circuit-level noise model, it achieves state-of-the-art performance with a very small average retained list size: less than 100 for the gross code $[[144,12,12]]$ at a physical error rate of $0.001$. When the list size is constant, the decoder has linear complexity, suggesting the possibility of low-latency implementations.
one_liner: Presents a pruned dynamic-programming decoder for quantum LDPC codes with near-optimal performance at small frontier sizes.
why_it_matters: Low-latency, high-performance decoding is a core bottleneck for fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The Frontier decoder processes sparse quantum decoding variables in an ordered dynamic-programming recursion, merging prefixes with identical residual syndrome and logical label. It approximates logical-coset posterior masses by retaining a narrow scored list, while the unpruned version is exact but exponentially costly. In code-capacity tests it approaches optimal thresholds for surface and color codes, and under circuit-level noise it reaches state-of-the-art performance with average retained list size below 100 for the [[144,12,12]] gross code at physical error rate 0.001.
main_result: Achieves strong decoding performance for sparse quantum codes using a narrow retained frontier, with linear complexity when list size is fixed.
application_angle: The approach points toward practical low-latency decoders for real-time fault-tolerant quantum computing control stacks.

title: Adiabatically-induced Kawaguchi geometry and jerk in quantum-classical systems
abstract: Adiabatically eliminating the quantum degrees of freedom in a mixed quantum-classical system produces an effective force in the classical equation of motion. The elimination can be made to any order in the adiabatic parameter, generating a series of higher order forces. By applying a sequence of near-identity unitary transformations to the quantum state, we derive a hierarchy of increasingly accurate effective actions for the classical variables. The third order Euler-Lagrange equation is non-Newtonian as the force depends on the jerk, the third order time derivative of position. We find that the third order terms induce a special kind of Kawaguchi geometry on the space of classical variables. This geometry is characterized by an almost symplectic structure and a differential line element that depends on the acceleration in addition to the velocity. Our results can be used to efficiently capture higher order nonadiabatic effects in molecular dynamics simulations.
